title: Chain Migration

jokes.text: The coordinated left/right confusion between George Sr. and Buster while chained together on a tandem bicycle
jokes.setup: Two men are chained together and trying to navigate a tandem bicycle, calling out directions
jokes.punchline: Their lefts and rights keep contradicting each other in escalating confusion: 'My left, your right' / 'Your left, my right' / 'My right, your right' / 'My right, my left'
jokes.explanation: The escalating left/right confusion is a well-structured physical comedy bit that uses the absurd premise of two men chained together on a bike. The repetition with variation works, and the narrator punctuating the action adds rhythm. Solid but not exceptional — the joke is the situation more than the writing.

jokes.text: Buster calculates Justin Bieber's age by referencing 'Justin Be-Bear' at Build-A-Bear
jokes.setup: Buster is trying to remember how long ago he went to Build-A-Bear to estimate whether it's still there
jokes.punchline: He calculates the timeline via 'Justin Be-Bear is seven,' a pun on Justin Bieber filtered through Buster's childlike, mall-obsessed worldview
jokes.explanation: This is a dense, layered joke that works on multiple levels: it's a wordplay pun (Justin Be-Bear/Justin Bieber), it's deeply character-specific (only Buster would track time via a Build-A-Bear stuffed animal named after a pop star), and it reveals Buster's arrested development (pun intended) — he's a grown man who frequents children's mall stores. The economy is excellent; the whole thing lands in one line. Could only come from this character. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: Lucille references Buster's favorite mall stores: 'Drink-A-Juice,' 'Paint-A-Plate,' 'Build-A-Bear'... 'Oh, there's a tiny train.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is explaining why she thinks the adult Buster would be at the mall
jokes.punchline: She lists children's activity stores as his favorites, culminating in spotting a tiny train with genuine excitement — revealing how infantilized Buster is in her mind (and in reality)
jokes.explanation: The escalating list of children's stores attributed to an adult man is funny on its own, but 'Oh, there's a tiny train' as the genuine capstone — delivered in real-time as she spots it — is the perfect button. It's deeply character-integrated: Lucille both created and enables Buster's arrested development, and her fond recognition of the train implicates her as much as it mocks him.

jokes.text: 'But don't you think he would've smelled me by now?' / 'Oh, good point. Maybe the Popcorn Plantation is masking your scent.'
jokes.setup: They're wondering why Buster hasn't found them yet in the mall
jokes.punchline: Lucille's concern is that Buster tracks her by scent like an animal; George Sr. accepts this premise completely and offers a rational explanation
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. The premise — that Buster tracks his mother by scent like an animal — is deeply specific to their pathological relationship established over seasons, yet it lands even cold. The structure is perfect: Lucille raises the question with apparent sincerity, George Sr. immediately accepts it as reasonable and offers the 'Popcorn Plantation' as a scientific countermeasure, then pivots to 'we gotta get you out of the food court' as urgent tactical advice. Every beat is earned. The absurdity is character-derived, not random. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Buster was missing, and George Sr. was eager to make it look like that was something he cared about... in order to woo his wife.'
jokes.setup: The episode frames George Sr.'s search for Buster as parental concern
jokes.punchline: The narrator flatly reveals his motivation is entirely self-serving — to impress Lucille — delivered with complete matter-of-factness
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deflation of parental sincerity is a reliable AD tool. The economy is excellent — maximum character skewering per word. The 'eager to make it look like' construction is sharp. Slightly less original because this narrative mode is the show's bread and butter, but it's well-executed here.

jokes.text: Lucille wants to make a stuffed bear from a wallet photo at Build-A-Bear to 'let Buster know authorities weren't after him and get a little dig in'
jokes.setup: They need to communicate with Buster without being conspicuous
jokes.punchline: Lucille's solution is a Build-A-Bear made from a wallet photo — which would simultaneously be a comfort object and, knowing Lucille, contain a passive-aggressive message
jokes.explanation: The fact that Lucille's 'sweet' gesture to comfort her fugitive son is filtered through Build-A-Bear and contains 'a little dig' is perfectly in character. The layering — comfort object + passive aggression + that it requires a wallet photo from a grown woman for a stuffed bear — is dense and funny.

jokes.text: The printer is so slow that the narrator explains 'the speed of the printer made it very clear why Two Hour Teeth went out of business'
jokes.setup: Michael has printed a map as evidence against his parents, and everyone waits impatiently
jokes.punchline: The store they're in is 'Two Hour Teeth' — a dentist/print shop — and the painfully slow printer explains the business failure
jokes.explanation: The absurd business name 'Two Hour Teeth' is already funny, but the payoff — that its printer validates the name and explains why it went out of business — is a tight, well-constructed joke. The economy is strong; the narrator delivers it with perfect flatness. Classic AD background worldbuilding that pays off as comedy.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Michael and George Michael impatiently watching the printer crawl, cutting to them shouting 'Come on! Come on!'
jokes.setup: The printer is described as agonizingly slow
jokes.punchline: The visual of two grown men desperately urging a printer forward, combined with the absurdity of the wait for dramatic evidence
jokes.explanation: A functional, relatable frustration gag that services the scene's pacing. The 'Come on!' beat is earned by the setup but doesn't add much comedic craft beyond the situation. Gets the laugh but isn't clever.

jokes.text: Tobias explaining that freeze-dried ground beef underwater 'tastes just as good as any hamburger I've ever had underwater'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to make freeze-dried food sound appealing in their Tent City situation
jokes.punchline: His qualifier — 'just as good as any hamburger I've ever had underwater' — implies he regularly eats hamburgers underwater, which is both absurd and perfectly Tobias
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly compact character joke. The comparison — 'just as good as any hamburger I've ever had underwater' — is absurd, but the genius is how nonchalantly it implies a rich history of Tobias eating hamburgers in aquatic settings. It's a single subordinate clause that opens a window onto an insane life. Only Tobias. The economy is exceptional; the absurdity is character-derived. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: Tobias's plan to steal a homeless man's shopping cart full of supplies 'when he leaves it'
jokes.setup: They've run out of food; Tobias spots a man with a shopping cart of supplies
jokes.punchline: Tobias announces his plan to steal from a homeless person with cheerful confidence, treating it as a perfectly reasonable survival strategy
jokes.explanation: Dark in premise — stealing from a homeless person — but the joke is really about Tobias's oblivious confidence and his complete unawareness that he IS the homeless person. Decent character beat, slightly underdeveloped.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Tobias had been squatting in the very chain store where his brother-in-law was now trying to repair a chain problem'
jokes.setup: Two separate storylines converge at a hardware/bike store
jokes.punchline: The narrator highlights that Tobias is in a 'chain store' while Michael/Buster deal with a 'chain problem' — triple wordplay: chain store (retail), chain store (squatting), leg chains/bike chain
jokes.explanation: Classic AD narrator wordplay at its best. 'Chain store' / 'chain problem' simultaneously describes the retail location, the squatting situation, and the literal leg/bike chain tangle — three meanings in one elegant construction. The economy is near-perfect. The structure of the convergence joke (two plotlines meeting, narrated with loaded language) is a signature AD technique executed with precision. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Am I crazy, or am I smelling popcorn and Chanel No. 5 butter?'
jokes.setup: Buster is at the hardware store dealing with the chain problem and smells something
jokes.punchline: He conflates his mother's perfume (Chanel No. 5) with popcorn butter — a callback to the earlier 'smelling mother' joke and his animal-like tracking ability
jokes.explanation: This is the payoff to the earlier 'scent tracking' joke, and it's beautifully constructed. 'Chanel No. 5 butter' is a perfect fusion of the two smells — elegant perfume and fast-food grease — and Buster delivering it as a genuine sensory question proves the earlier joke right. The callback structure is airtight, the line is highly quotable, and only Buster can say it. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'These are stage manacles. We were in a play.'
jokes.setup: The hardware store worker asks about their leg chains
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately improvises that they're in a play — a plausible-enough lie that Buster delivers with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A quick, sharp improvisation line. 'Stage manacles' is a funny construction — the specificity of 'stage' before 'manacles' sells it. It also works on a meta level: the show is always theatrical. Buster's sincere delivery of absurd cover stories is a character staple. Well-executed.

jokes.text: Worker reveals the manacles were never locked — they just hadn't tried unclamping them
jokes.setup: The men have been chained together all episode, trying elaborate solutions to get free
jokes.punchline: 'Have you guys even tried just unclamping?' — they hadn't, and the manacles open immediately
jokes.explanation: A classic comedic payoff: the solution to all their effort was trivially simple and they never tried it. The structure is excellent — the setup runs for over three minutes, making the deflation maximally satisfying. 'Are you kidding me? We didn't even check.' earns the laugh through sheer earned frustration. The joke also works as character comedy — these men's incompetence is perfectly consistent.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Ironically, finding out they weren't shackled was a bonding moment for the men.'
jokes.setup: They've just discovered they were never actually chained together
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes that this humiliating shared incompetence somehow brought them closer
jokes.explanation: The narrator's ironic framing of shared embarrassment as bonding is a clean, well-delivered line. Standard AD narrator irony, executed with good economy.

jokes.text: 'That would've saved time when we were trying to get the pants over our heads.'
jokes.setup: Buster and Oscar are reflecting on the now-revealed fact that the manacles were never locked
jokes.punchline: Buster mentions trying to get pants over their heads — implying an off-screen sequence of two chained men attempting to change pants together — which is both horrifying and hilarious
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest jokes. The entire pants-over-heads ordeal happened off-screen, and Buster mentions it in passing as though it were a minor inconvenience — which implies a scene of spectacular physical comedy that the audience never saw. The economy is extraordinary: one subordinate clause conjures an entire absurd sequence. The rewatch value is high because on second viewing you realize how much chaos was implied. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: The strategic discussion of whether it's 'safer to go their separate ways' now that the manacles are off — treated as a tactical decision by two men who were never fugitives
jokes.setup: Oscar and Buster consider whether they should separate now that they're 'free'
jokes.punchline: They're treating the situation as a genuine fugitive tactical problem when in reality they were never in danger — the comedy comes from the seriousness applied to a non-situation
jokes.explanation: Functional irony — serious tactical language applied to a non-threat — but the joke isn't particularly sharp here. The scene is transitional, and the comedy comes more from accumulated character context than from the specific writing.

jokes.text: 'Well, realistically I could sniff out Mother somehow, but she wouldn't want me. Maybe I'm better in prison. I'm no good on my own.'
jokes.setup: Buster is pondering where to go if they separate
jokes.punchline: He considers 'sniffing out Mother' (confirming the animal-tracking dynamic), immediately self-deprecates that she wouldn't want him, and concludes prison might be his best option — all delivered as sincere self-assessment
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully dark, character-dense line. 'I could sniff out Mother somehow' confirms the animal-behavior joke from earlier; 'she wouldn't want me' is devastating (because it's true, and Buster knows it); 'maybe I'm better in prison' is the darkest possible punchline, delivered without irony. All three beats land. Deep character integration.

jokes.text: The 'Inside-Outside Bandits' debate: Buster wants to be outside, Oscar wants to be inside — treated as a fundamental compatibility issue
jokes.setup: Oscar and Buster are trying to find common ground for where to spend their time together
jokes.punchline: Their irreconcilable difference is literally 'inside vs. outside,' mirroring an old-couple sitcom dynamic but also reflecting their literal prison/freedom circumstances — then they find a compromise: a tent that 'feels like we're outside / feels like we're inside'
jokes.explanation: The inside/outside debate has multiple layers: it's a literal preference conflict, a metaphor for their prison/free status, and a setup for the tent payoff. The show name (Arrested Development) resonates with the Buster's need for indoor safety. Clever construction with good thematic resonance.

jokes.text: 'The most important thing is that we're outside.' / 'Inside.' / 'Outside.' / 'Inside.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. says what matters is being outside; Buster corrects him because he prefers being inside
jokes.punchline: The running 'Inside-Outside Bandits' temperamental mismatch — the men who escaped prison can't agree on whether they prefer indoor or outdoor spaces
jokes.explanation: Sets up the 'Inside-Outside Bandits' concept efficiently. The joke is in the repetition and the irony that men who went to extreme lengths to escape find themselves disagreeing on whether outside is even desirable. Character integration is high for Buster specifically.

jokes.text: The printer finally finishes and the map has 'Make Background Tongue' enabled, producing a map of a tongue instead of a border wall
jokes.setup: Michael has waited an agonizing amount of time for a dramatic piece of evidence to print
jokes.punchline: The printed map has a tongue superimposed on it because they forgot to uncheck 'Make Background Tongue' — an absurd software option that ruins the evidence and explains why Two Hour Teeth failed
jokes.explanation: 'Make Background Tongue' is a spectacularly absurd piece of software fiction — it's plausible enough (printers do have background settings) but the specific option being 'tongue' ties back to the dental business, explains why the business failed, and visually undermines Michael's dramatic moment. Dense, well-constructed, pays off multiple setups. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Oscar and Buster resolve their inside/outside dispute by moving into a Tent City display tent: 'Feels like we're outside / It feels like we're inside'
jokes.setup: They need somewhere that meets both their needs
jokes.punchline: A display tent in what appears to be a sporting goods section of the chain store solves the problem — each perceives it differently based on their preference
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the inside/outside dispute. The tent solution is elegantly absurd — two fugitives living in a store display — and the synchronized but opposite perceptions ('feels outside / feels inside') is a perfect button. Good structure.

jokes.text: 'You're looking at a boy who faked a coma for two weeks to get out of a spelling bee.'
jokes.setup: Oscar asks if Buster can pretend to be a mannequin to avoid detection
jokes.punchline: Buster confirms his ability to stay still by referencing faking a coma to avoid a spelling bee — an escalating absurdity that reveals a lifetime of extreme avoidance behaviors
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode and arguably in the season. 'Faked a coma for two weeks to get out of a spelling bee' is perfectly calibrated absurdity: the stakes are wildly disproportionate (coma vs. spelling bee), the duration is specific (two weeks — not one, not three), it's entirely consistent with everything we know about Buster, and Oscar immediately corrects him ('That was to get out of testifying') — revealing there was ALSO a real coma that lasted three months. The one-line construction opens an abyss of implied backstory. Deeply character-specific. Extraordinarily quotable. Justifies 9+.

jokes.text: Oscar corrects: 'That was to get out of testifying.' / 'No, that was a three-month coma.'
jokes.setup: Buster claims he faked a coma for two weeks to get out of a spelling bee
jokes.punchline: Oscar clarifies the coma Buster is thinking of was actually for testifying — revealing that Buster has faked MULTIPLE comas for different purposes, including an apparently real three-month coma
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. The escalation from 'faked a coma for a spelling bee' to 'no, THAT coma was for testifying — there's ALSO a real three-month coma' is a textbook escalation that gets funnier with each piece of new information. We're left with: Buster faked a coma to avoid a spelling bee (two weeks), faked a different coma to avoid testifying, AND had a real three-month coma — all separate incidents. The economy is perfect; the delivery (Oscar's mild correction, Buster's blasé 'no, that was a three-month coma') implies complete normalcy. This is Arrested Development at its densest and funniest. Comparable to 'snip, snap' level craft — the accumulation creates something greater than its parts.

jokes.text: Buster's pre-departure speech to Oscar devolves into 'I cherish it' after multiple false starts
jokes.setup: Buster is trying to express genuine emotion before potentially being caught
jokes.punchline: After multiple 'this... this... being with you has been... ah, it's been...' false starts, he lands on the single word 'I cherish it' — perfectly Buster, both touching and comically deflated
jokes.explanation: The comedic structure of the false starts is classic — the audience expects a heartfelt speech and gets a single clipped word. 'I cherish it' is the perfect choice: slightly formal, slightly odd, completely Buster. The 'My son.' response from Oscar adds a separate layer. Well-structured emotional deflation.

jokes.text: Oscar responds to Buster's 'I cherish it' with a simple 'My son.' — followed by 'All right, fine. Be that way.'
jokes.setup: Buster has just had a vulnerable moment saying 'I cherish it'
jokes.punchline: Oscar's 'My son.' is a weirdly formal, almost cinematic response — then the emotional moment is immediately deflated when Oscar says 'All right, fine. Be that way.' as though it was Buster who was being withholding
jokes.explanation: 'All right, fine. Be that way.' after his own non-responsive 'My son.' is a funny inversion — Oscar acts aggrieved at Buster's emotional restraint despite having just delivered an equally opaque response. The deflation of the tender moment is well-timed.

jokes.text: 'It makes you not even want to do business with a hostile nation.'
jokes.setup: Lucille and George Sr. are panicking because the Chinese investors are coming for their money back
jokes.punchline: Lucille delivers the line as though the lesson here is about the inconvenience of international business deals, not about the ethical problem of taking money from a hostile government to build a border wall
jokes.explanation: A sharp political-comedy line that works as character comedy too. Lucille's complaint that dealing with a hostile nation is simply 'inconvenient' is a perfect skewering of her total moral blindness and the absurdity of the show's wall-building subplot. The understatement is the joke — she frames geopolitical fraud as a scheduling annoyance. Quotable.

jokes.text: George Sr. keeps pivoting back to the tandem bicycle: 'Know what looks like fun? That tandem bicycle built for two. How much fun would that be?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is ostensibly worried about the Chinese investor dinner but keeps fixating on the tandem bike
jokes.punchline: His obsession with the tandem bike as a symbol of reconnection with Lucille is absurdly misplaced given the crisis they're in — 'Boy, would we have fun on that'
jokes.explanation: The tandem bike fixation is a running gag that works as character comedy — George Sr.'s misguided romantic gestures are consistent — but this particular iteration isn't especially sharp. The joke is clearer in the later payoff.

jokes.text: George Sr. finds the 'more fun version of himself' at the Tent City — it's his twin brother Oscar
jokes.setup: Narrator says George Sr. 'found the more fun version of himself'
jokes.punchline: Cut to Oscar — his identical twin — as the literal answer
jokes.explanation: A clean narrator misdirect: 'the more fun version of himself' sounds like George Sr. found an inner joy, then reveals it's literally his twin. The economy is good and the structure (narrator line → visual reveal) is tight. Standard-good AD narrator joke.

jokes.text: George Sr. gives Buster a 'missing' poster as proof he's a free man — 'They throw these in with the posters'
jokes.setup: Oscar needs to convince Buster he's not being chased and presents the missing poster as proof
jokes.punchline: The 'proof' that authorities aren't after Buster is... a missing poster with his face on it — the exact opposite of reassurance — with the dismissive 'they throw these in with the posters'
jokes.explanation: The logical inversion is good — a missing person poster presented as evidence that no one is looking for you — and 'they throw these in with the posters' implies some bizarre promotional bundle. The deadpan logic is well-constructed.

jokes.text: 'Actually, he's not technically free. He still has to officially sign out from prison and pay their $75 process fee.'
jokes.setup: Oscar has just told Buster he's a free man
jokes.punchline: George Sr. clarifies: Buster's freedom requires a $75 sign-out fee — bureaucratic absurdity that makes imprisonment sound like a gym membership cancellation
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '$75' is perfect — not a round number, not dramatic, just a petty bureaucratic fee that equates prison release with a transaction. The economy is excellent; 'sign out from prison' sounds like leaving a hotel. Sharp absurdist bureaucracy joke.

jokes.text: Oscar reveals he's trying to extend the 'Buster's on the run' charade to give George Sr. more bonding time with Lucille — for a $75 fee
jokes.setup: Oscar could tell Buster he's free; instead he's negotiating with George Sr. to keep the charade going
jokes.punchline: The elaborate emotional manipulation of an entire family is being orchestrated for $75 — the pettiness of the transaction undercuts the drama
jokes.explanation: The callback to the $75 fee as the entire price of the deception is funny in its smallness. The show's running theme of elaborate schemes for petty stakes is well-served here.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'But before Oscar could even not share the news with Buster that they weren't on the run, Tobias would not solve a mystery he wasn't trying to solve himself.'
jokes.setup: Transitioning between storylines
jokes.punchline: The narrator constructs a sentence of double negatives that precisely describes the convoluted state of everyone's knowledge — 'not share,' 'weren't on the run,' 'would not solve,' 'wasn't trying to solve'
jokes.explanation: A tour-de-force of layered negatives that's very AD — the narrator's precision about who doesn't know what creates comedy through its own logical intricacy. The sentence is almost impossible to parse on first listen but is technically accurate, which is the joke. High craft, moderate impact because it requires re-processing.

jokes.text: Tobias wanders into the display tent and apologizes to 'a mannequin' before recognizing Buster
jokes.setup: Buster has been posing as a mannequin in the store display
jokes.punchline: Tobias stumbles in, starts apologizing to what he thinks is a mannequin, then recognizes Buster — the scene plays both as a visual gag (Buster successfully passed as a mannequin) and a character joke (Tobias is oblivious enough to wander into occupied tents)
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the mannequin setup works well, especially as a callback to Buster's coma-faking backstory. The visual comedy of Buster fooling Tobias while Tobias politely apologizes to a 'mannequin' is solid. Character integration is good — both Buster's stillness and Tobias's obliviousness are established traits.

jokes.text: Tobias walks into a tent, sees what he thinks is a mannequin, begins apologizing to it, then realizes: 'What am I doing, apologizing to a mannequin that looks like... Buster?'
jokes.setup: Tobias has walked into the wrong tent; Buster is pretending to be a mannequin
jokes.punchline: Tobias begins apologizing to what he believes is a mannequin — then does a double take when he realizes it's Buster, and his confusion is further compounded because the situation requires him to question why a mannequin looks like Buster
jokes.explanation: A clean physical-visual payoff for the mannequin setup. The joke works because Buster is apparently convincing enough that Tobias — who KNOWS Buster — walks past him before the realization kicks in. Good structure.

jokes.text: 'Rumor has it they're gonna knock down these tents and put in a Santa Ski Village.' / 'Seems like we were just celebrating the second of July. We're already talking about December 19.'
jokes.setup: Tobias mentions the mall tents will be replaced by a Santa Ski Village
jokes.punchline: Buster's response treats 'December 19' as a specific date worth lamenting — 'seems like we were just celebrating the second of July' — a take on 'time flies' that's specifically bizarre in its date choices (not July 4, not December 25)
jokes.explanation: The choice of 'the second of July' and 'December 19' instead of July 4th and Christmas is a subtle but sharp joke — these are the days adjacent to the holidays, slightly off, which is perfectly Buster (close but not quite). It also rewards rewatching when you notice the specificity. Clean observational-adjacent absurdism.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'She should get better acquainted with her sibling and mother impersonator.'
jokes.setup: Tobias learns his daughter Maeby has a place at the Spotted Palm retirement community
jokes.punchline: He describes Lucille 2 as Maeby's 'sibling and mother impersonator' — acknowledging the show's convoluted family structure in a single absurd but accurate descriptor
jokes.explanation: Dense family-structure joke that requires knowledge of the show's convoluted genealogy. 'Sibling and mother impersonator' packs a lot of AD lore into a short phrase. Rewards dedicated viewers.

jokes.text: Tobias responds to the '55 and older' age restriction: 'Well, that sounds like a challenge to any family that wasn't a ragtag troupe of folks whom I wish to become actors.'
jokes.setup: The Spotted Palm retirement community requires residents to be 55+; Tobias and his family are not
jokes.punchline: Tobias frames the age restriction as a theatrical challenge for his 'ragtag troupe' — simultaneously revealing his plan to disguise his family as elderly people and his delusional actor-director self-concept
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias: turns any obstacle into a theatrical opportunity and frames his family as his troupe. 'Ragtag troupe of folks whom I wish to become actors' is perfectly awkward phrasing that captures his grandiose delusions. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Maeby is gaslighting Stan about how frequently he's asked the same questions — 'Every month' (he hadn't; 'in fact, this had only happened a few hours earlier')
jokes.setup: Stan has memory issues after an accident and asks if he's asked these questions before
jokes.punchline: Maeby says 'every month' and the narrator clarifies it's only been a few hours — she's actively gaslighting a head-injured elderly man for housing
jokes.explanation: Dark but very AD: Maeby exploiting a vulnerable person's amnesia is morally outrageous, which is exactly the show's register. The narrator's clinical correction ('in fact, this had only happened a few hours earlier') makes it funnier by confirming the gaslighting without comment. The 'you always say right' / 'right' / 'right. See?' loop is a nice structural touch.

jokes.text: Maeby's dementia con — she has been gaslighting Stan into believing he has memory problems, repeating conversations to make him question his mind
jokes.setup: Stan asks if his headache is better; Maeby acts exasperated as if he's asked this 'every month'
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals it had only happened once before — Maeby is actively constructing a false reality to manipulate an elderly man with a head injury
jokes.explanation: Dark and effective — the show treats Maeby gaslighting an injured elderly man as comedy, which is very Arrested Development. The structure (narrator revealing it had only happened once) is elegant. Dark subversive comedy with real craft behind it.

jokes.text: Maeby's breakup speech is interrupted: 'A real lady lets it itch. She doesn't jam two fingers...' / 'Voices up.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is delivering what sounds like a heartfelt breakup speech to Stan
jokes.punchline: The speech is interrupted mid-sentence by what sounds like crude advice ('she doesn't jam two fingers...'), then 'Voices up' — suggesting we've cut to a different scene mid-sentence, or someone is overhearing and commenting, creating an abrupt comedic interruption
jokes.explanation: The abrupt mid-sentence cut from 'she doesn't jam two fingers' to 'Voices up' is a classic AD misdirection — the truncated vulgarity works as an implied joke while 'Voices up' transitions into the Mrs. Featherbottom scene. The editing-as-comedy is smart.

jokes.text: Tobias enters as 'Mrs. Featherbottom' singing the Golden Girls theme, accompanied by 'Mrs. Bettywhite'
jokes.setup: Tobias needs to infiltrate the 55+ retirement community at the Spotted Palm
jokes.punchline: He's deployed his recurring Mrs. Featherbottom disguise (a female British housekeeper), now upgraded with 'Mrs. Bettywhite' as a companion, singing the Golden Girls theme — three jokes in one: recurring disguise callback, the Betty White name pun, and the Golden Girls as infiltration strategy for a retirement community
jokes.explanation: The return of Mrs. Featherbottom is always high-impact for AD fans, and this deployment is particularly clever: the Golden Girls theme as the infiltration song for a retirement community is perfectly logical within the show's absurdist framework. 'Mrs. Bettywhite' as a companion name is a delightful pun. The layers — recurring character, musical entrance, name joke, situational logic — make this a standout moment. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'We're just three... 56-year-old women looking for a place to rest our weary asses.'
jokes.setup: Tobias (as Mrs. Featherbottom) is introducing himself and accomplices to the Spotted Palm
jokes.punchline: The pause before '56-year-old' (recalibrating above the 55 threshold) and 'weary asses' as the closing descriptor — Tobias's disguise barely conceals his own speech patterns
jokes.explanation: The computational pause before '56' (just above the 55 minimum) is a lovely character beat — Tobias thinking on his feet while maintaining the disguise. 'Weary asses' as a conclusion is incongruously casual and crude for the otherwise genteel performance. Good character integration.

jokes.text: 'I think I can eat a piece of fish without giving away how I loathe you.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is coaching Lucille on how to behave at the Chinese investor dinner
jokes.punchline: Lucille's confident claim that she can suppress her loathing of her husband for one meal — delivered as though this is a modest but achievable goal
jokes.explanation: Lucille at her sharpest. The specificity of 'a piece of fish' as the unit of time she can manage her loathing is inspired — it's not 'a dinner,' it's one piece of fish. The casual confidence of 'how I loathe you' (not dislike, not resent — loathe) while treating it as a minor social challenge is perfect Lucille. Highly quotable. Could only be this character. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Whoops. Now, see? It's those little inadvertent slips I fear will come out tomorrow night if we go in cold.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has just declared she loathes George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. treats 'I loathe you' as a minor 'inadvertent slip' that could embarrass them at dinner — the comedy is his complete normalization of his wife's open hatred
jokes.explanation: 'Inadvertent slips' treating open loathing as a social faux pas is a good deadpan follow-up to Lucille's line. The structure (her brutality → his mild concern framing) is classic George/Lucille dynamic.

jokes.text: George Sr.'s backstory: he failed at 'ocean balling' to impress Lucille and 'gerbilled up to the beach' controlled by the Newport Bay Boys
jokes.setup: The narrator explains how George Sr. first encountered Dusty Radler
jokes.punchline: 'Gerbilled up to the beach' — an invented verb implying George Sr. arrived inside an ocean ball like a gerbil in a hamster ball — the specificity and casualness of the invented word is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Gerbilled up to the beach' is a wonderful AD neologism — the narrator using 'gerbilled' as a verb of motion implies such complete normalcy around George Sr.'s humiliation that it has its own vocabulary. The specificity of 'ocean balling' as a sport George would attempt to impress his wife also opens a rich comic world.

jokes.text: Newport Bay Boys refuse to let George Sr. use their steps: 'Those steps are just for the feet of the Bay Boys. Get back in the ball, dude.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is trapped in an ocean ball on a beach controlled by a surf gang
jokes.punchline: The gang has specific rules about stair usage ('just for the feet of the Bay Boys') and enforce George Sr.'s re-entry into the ball with casual authority
jokes.explanation: The territorial specificity of the steps ('just for the feet of the Bay Boys') combined with the casual 'Get back in the ball, dude' is well-timed absurdism. George Sr. trapped in an ocean ball while negotiating with surf gang bureaucracy is a solid comedic situation.

jokes.text: George Sr. (from inside the ball): 'Could you tell Lucille that I... I died... doing something that looked fun?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has been forced back into his ocean ball by the surf gang
jokes.punchline: His final request — that they tell Lucille he died doing 'something that looked fun' — is a perfectly pathetic encapsulation of his entire arc: he doesn't need to actually be having fun, just to appear to be
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional character line. 'Something that looked fun' — not 'something fun,' but 'something that LOOKED fun' — perfectly encapsulates George Sr.'s performative vanity. He wants the appearance of a good time more than the thing itself. The delivery from inside a beach ball adds the perfect physical comedy context. High quotability and character integration.

jokes.text: George Sr. asks Dusty to tell Lucille 'that I died... doing something that looked fun'
jokes.setup: Trapped in the ocean ball with no exit, George Sr. asks for a message to be passed to Lucille
jokes.punchline: His requested eulogy is 'doing something that looked fun' — not that it WAS fun, just that it LOOKED fun — a perfectly George Sr. self-presentation concern even in hypothetical death
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. George Sr.'s dying concern is not his family or his legacy, but whether his death appeared fun to onlookers — and crucially, 'looked fun,' not 'was fun.' The distinction is devastating: he doesn't care about experiencing fun, only performing it. This is deeply consistent with his character across the series. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: 'This is the goddamn expert? Because the only thing he's expert at is getting sand out of his trunks and pissing on another man's tree.'
jokes.setup: Lucille presents Dusty as an expert on Eastern culture
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s furious itemization of Dusty's actual expertise: sand removal and territorial urination
jokes.explanation: 'Pissing on another man's tree' is an evocative and unexpected specificity. The list format (sand + urination + the law + guitar) is structurally sound and George Sr.'s territorial jealousy gives it character grounding. Solid if not exceptional.

jokes.text: Dusty: 'And the law.' / 'And I play some guitar.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has just savaged Dusty's supposed expertise
jokes.punchline: Dusty calmly adds two corrections to George Sr.'s dismissal — he's also an expert in 'the law' and plays guitar — neither of which makes him more qualified for the Far East cultural consultation
jokes.explanation: The deadpan additions — delivered with complete equanimity — are very funny. 'And the law' sounds absurd as a skill credential, and 'And I play some guitar' as an afterthought is a perfect comedic button. Economy is the main strength here.

jokes.text: 'Oh, you served in Vietnam?' / 'Surfed. I surfed in Vietnam. Two group tours.'
jokes.setup: Dusty mentions he 'surfed in 'Nam' as expertise in Eastern culture
jokes.punchline: George Sr. mishears 'served,' and Dusty clarifies: he literally surfed in Vietnam as a tourist ('Two group tours') — not a veteran but a surf tourist
jokes.explanation: A perfectly structured served/surfed pun. The setup is natural (the words are phonetically similar), the misdirect (assuming military service) is earned, and 'Two group tours' as the clarification — implying he went back for more tourist surfing in a war zone/former war zone — is a spectacular escalation. The economy is near-perfect: maximum information per word. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'We went for sushi last night, and he never touched his fork.' / 'Well, a little bit for the rice. It's the hard part. But not when the guy was looking.'
jokes.setup: Lucille cites Dusty's sushi fork abstinence as proof of his Eastern cultural expertise
jokes.punchline: Dusty immediately qualifies: he DID use the fork, just for rice, and only when the waiter wasn't looking — completely undermining the claim
jokes.explanation: 'Not when the guy was looking' is the perfect button — it reframes Dusty's 'cultural respect' as basic performance anxiety about being judged by sushi restaurant staff. The escalating qualification structure (never touched fork → well, for rice → but not when watched) is beautifully timed. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Dusty's 'cultural expertise': 'Picked up a lot, too. Mostly tchotchkes, but some cultural elements, too.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is describing what he learned from his surf tours to Vietnam
jokes.punchline: His primary cultural takeaway was 'tchotchkes' — tourist trinkets — with 'some cultural elements' as an afterthought, suggesting the trinkets are the main event
jokes.explanation: 'Mostly tchotchkes, but some cultural elements, too' is a beautifully constructed sentence. The tchotchkes are the main category; culture is the exception. It perfectly captures the oblivious tourist who thinks gift shopping is cultural immersion.

jokes.text: Dusty's Eastern culture philosophy: 'They all are interested in honor... your opponent needs to save face, for this is the only way you, yourself, will save face.' Then: 'If you think that I enjoy... intimidating you, emasculating you, you're wrong, little guy.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is delivering what sounds like genuine cultural wisdom about saving face
jokes.punchline: He immediately demonstrates the exact opposite — calling George Sr. 'little guy' and listing all the ways he enjoys dominating him — while ostensibly explaining why he doesn't enjoy it
jokes.explanation: The irony of Dusty explaining 'saving face' while actively humiliating George Sr. ('little guy') in the same breath is well-constructed. The list of humiliations ('intimidating... emasculating') followed by 'you're wrong, little guy' is a solid structural irony.

jokes.text: Dusty's Far East philosophy: 'With all these Asia-y cultures and, uh, whatnot, they all are interested in honor... your opponent needs to save face, for this is the only way you, yourself, will save face.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is sharing his Far East cultural wisdom with the Bluths
jokes.punchline: His 'wisdom' is a vague, slightly condescending summary of pan-Asian culture ('all these Asia-y cultures and whatnot') that manages to be both generic and slightly offensive, delivered with complete confidence
jokes.explanation: 'Asia-y cultures and whatnot' is a brilliant coinage — it's exactly how someone with zero actual knowledge but total confidence would characterize an entire continent's cultures. The delivery of face-saving wisdom as profound insight, immediately undercut by its delivery context, is solid.

jokes.text: Dusty exits to 'watch the big TV' upstairs — implying George Sr.'s TV is so small it's worth mentioning
jokes.setup: George Sr. asks Dusty to give him a moment alone with Lucille
jokes.punchline: Dusty says he'll go watch 'the big TV' — the qualifier 'big' implies he knows where both TVs are, suggesting familiarity with the household that emasculates George Sr. further
jokes.explanation: The 'big TV' detail implies a small TV elsewhere in the house that George Sr. uses — a quiet emasculation beat. The casual ownership Dusty demonstrates over the household is a subtle visual/character gag.

jokes.text: Michael presents his wall evidence: 'This was a file on a computer drive called Gated Community.'
jokes.setup: Michael has found damning evidence of his parents building a border wall
jokes.punchline: The file is named 'Gated Community' — a barely-disguised euphemism that the Bluths presumably thought was clever concealment
jokes.explanation: 'Gated Community' as the filename is pitch-perfect Bluth hubris — they think they're being subtle but the euphemism barely qualifies as one. It's also a genuine real-estate double meaning (gated community = upscale neighborhood = border wall). Efficient and pointed.

jokes.text: 'This is a border wall between Mexico and America.' / 'That doesn't look like a mouth to you?'
jokes.setup: Michael presents the printed map as evidence of a border wall
jokes.punchline: Lucille responds by asking if it looks like a mouth — a callback to the 'Make Background Tongue' misprint, which Michael apparently can't explain away despite knowing the tongue was a printing error
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the tongue/map joke: the very misprint that humiliated Michael now genuinely undercuts his dramatic confrontation. Lucille's 'That doesn't look like a mouth to you?' is devastating because she's right — the evidence is visually compromised. Michael's frustration ('you gotta get rid of the tongue setting') is the final beat. Beautifully constructed callback.

jokes.text: 'There's Chinese Romneys?' / 'No, the only Romneys are the Mexican Romneys.' / 'Well, and the regular Romneys.'
jokes.setup: The Bluths reveal they took Chinese money for the wall, and George Sr. mentions the 'Mexican Romney' won't refund them
jokes.punchline: Michael's confusion about whether there are Chinese Romneys escalates into a definitive taxonomy of Romneys: Mexican Romneys, regular Romneys — a deadpan categorization of a real family as though they're a franchise
jokes.explanation: The Romney taxonomy joke is absurdist gold. The premise — that Mitt Romney has a Mexican branch of the family (a real fact, actually, which makes it more absurd) — is treated with complete matter-of-factness. 'No, the only Romneys are the Mexican Romneys' followed by '...well, and the regular Romneys' as a concession is perfectly structured. The three-beat exchange is economical and escalating. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'The Mexican Romney said no refundo.' / 'They're Chinese.' / 'There's Chinese Romneys?' / 'No, the only Romneys are the Mexican Romneys.' / 'Well, and the regular Romneys.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains why the deal collapsed — 'the Mexican Romney said no refundo'
jokes.punchline: A rapid-fire exchange escalates the absurdity: confusion about whether the investors are Chinese or Mexican, the existence of 'Mexican Romneys' as an established fact, confusion about 'Chinese Romneys,' and the clarification that 'regular Romneys' also exist — creating an entire universe of Romney family ethnicity variants
jokes.explanation: A beautifully escalating absurdist joke that creates its own internal logic. 'Mexican Romney' is a great concept (a reference to Mitt Romney's family's Mexican heritage twisted into a character), 'no refundo' is a fake-Spanish legal term, and the rapid-fire expansion into Chinese Romneys and the acknowledgment of 'regular Romneys' builds a whole mythology. Highly quotable and rewards discussion.

jokes.text: 'Shrillary.' [Beat] 'You okay? Is everybody all right?' 'Yeah, we're fine. Go on.' 'Right on.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. invokes Hillary Clinton's voice in an argument, says 'Shrillary'
jokes.punchline: Dusty immediately checks if everyone's okay — as if 'Shrillary' were a dangerous sound or a code word — then exits with 'Right on' after being reassured, completely unnervingly casual about the interruption
jokes.explanation: 'Shrillary' is a dated political pun that lands weakly as a joke in itself — it's relying on a cultural reference without adding a twist. The saving grace is Dusty's reaction beat ('You okay?') treating it as a possible emergency, which adds a layer. But the earned_vs_cheap is low because the 'Shrillary' pun is lazy.

jokes.text: Michael's advice: 'You made a mistake. You lost the money. You feel bad. Nothing you can do. You hope they understand.' / 'I have no idea what you're talking about.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells his father to just be honest with the Chinese investors
jokes.punchline: George Sr. responds 'I have no idea what you're talking about' — not as a genuine denial of the advice, but as his practiced non-response to any suggestion of honesty or accountability
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s reflexive denial even of advice — not of facts, but of the concept of honesty — is deeply character-integrated. 'I have no idea what you're talking about' as a response to 'just tell the truth' is a perfect encapsulation of the Bluth patriarch.

jokes.text: 'This generation. They go to one therapy session, and all of a sudden their parents are terrible people, because they once sold tract homes to... God, who even remembers now?' / 'Saddam Hussein.' / 'Yeah. So... I mean, something like that.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is dismissing Michael's moral concerns as therapy-induced oversensitivity
jokes.punchline: His 'who even remembers' vagueness about selling homes to Saddam Hussein — followed by Michael's flat 'Saddam Hussein' — and George Sr.'s breezy 'yeah, something like that' treating it as a minor biographical detail
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's strongest jokes. The structure is perfect: George Sr.'s therapy critique builds to 'who even remembers' as a cover for something monstrous; Michael's flat 'Saddam Hussein' is delivered with zero theatrics; George Sr.'s 'yeah, so, I mean, something like that' treats war-criminal commerce as a minor footnote. The economy is extraordinary — three short lines contain layers of character history, moral bankruptcy, and comedic escalation. The callback to established show lore deepens the impact. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Buster's hand 'flew off' on the 405 freeway during their getaway
jokes.setup: Oscar and Buster are recounting their getaway drive; Oscar mentions a complication
jokes.punchline: 'It didn't help things when your hand flew off on the 405' — Buster's prosthetic hook/hand detaching on a major freeway, mentioned casually as a traffic complication
jokes.explanation: Buster's hook/prosthetic hand is a long-running character element. 'Your hand flew off on the 405' — the casualness of the location detail (a specific freeway), the verb 'flew off,' and Buster's unconcerned 'it's probably behind the seat' — is a perfect dark-comedy beat. Only possible with this character. Very quotable within the show's universe.

jokes.text: 'Welcome Home From Prison Again, Buster' banner — interpreted as ironic
jokes.setup: Buster notices a banner welcoming him home from prison
jokes.punchline: Oscar explains it's 'ironic' — 'like a not-surprise party' — Buster is amazed his mother went through the trouble, Oscar notes 'I guess it worked'
jokes.explanation: The 'not-surprise party' explanation is a delightful bit of Buster/Oscar logic — the tortured definition of ironic gets its own mini-Abbott-and-Costello structure, and 'I guess it worked' as the conclusion (he IS surprised) is a clean button.

jokes.text: Oscar's explanation of a 'not-surprise party': 'Like if Buster was coming home, he'd expect a surprise party, so it wouldn't be a surprise. Since he's in jail, we're gonna throw a party and not tell him since there won't be guests.'
jokes.setup: Oscar is explaining the logic of the 'Welcome Home From Prison Again' banner to a confused Buster
jokes.punchline: Oscar's explanation of a 'not-surprise party' becomes increasingly circular and finally arrives at 'there won't be guests' — a party without guests for a man in jail, as a tribute to the concept of a party
jokes.explanation: Oscar's koan-like explanation of party logic is genuinely absurdist — the party gets more and more ethereal as he explains it until it evaporates entirely. The show's tradition of characters confidently explaining nonsense is well-served here.

jokes.text: 'A guy could have a mighty nice life up here.' / 'Do you... You want us to die here together?'
jokes.setup: Oscar is enthusiastically describing life in the attic as cozy and comfortable
jokes.punchline: Buster interprets Oscar's contentment as a plan to live in the attic until they die — and asks about it directly and without horror
jokes.explanation: 'Do you want us to die here together?' asked without alarm — just as clarifying information — is a beautifully dark character beat. Buster has so little sense of his own life's value that dying in an attic registers as a reasonable outcome to be confirmed, not resisted. Deep character integration.

jokes.text: Buster's solution to attic hunger: 'How does gummies sound?' / 'Necessary.'
jokes.setup: Oscar says he'll go get food; Buster responds
jokes.punchline: 'Necessary' — Oscar's single-word response treating gummy bears as a medical necessity is both deadpan and perfectly calibrated to their degraded situation
jokes.explanation: 'Necessary' as a response to 'How does gummies sound?' is maximally economical — one word that implies physical and emotional dependency on candy while treating it as survival-level sustenance. Perfect deadpan.

jokes.text: Buster refuses Gob's favor: 'I cannot do you a favor, Gob. I am on the lam. I escaped from jail.'
jokes.setup: Gob asks Buster for help with a favor
jokes.punchline: Buster refuses on the grounds that he is 'on the lam' and 'escaped from jail' — both of which are false, but Buster doesn't know that yet
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is functional — the audience knows Buster isn't a fugitive. Competent but not exceptional; the joke relies entirely on the dramatic irony which is well-established at this point.

jokes.text: Gob: 'They released you, Buster.' Buster: 'I don't think so. Uh, I think I'd know if I wasn't on the lam.'
jokes.setup: Gob tells Buster he was released from prison, not on the run
jokes.punchline: Buster's epistemic confidence — 'I think I'd know if I wasn't on the lam' — is the exact inverse of self-awareness; he's been wrong this entire time and is certain he would know if he were wrong
jokes.explanation: 'I think I'd know if I wasn't on the lam' is a wonderful Dunning-Kruger line — his confidence is exactly proportional to his ignorance. The structure (Gob's flat statement, Buster's confident denial, the dramatic irony that he's wrong) is well-constructed. Deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: 'Impossible. You weren't even a wanted child.' / [Buster realizes the poster's meaning]
jokes.setup: Buster insists he must be a wanted man because he's been on the run
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'Impossible. You weren't even a wanted child.' — a devastating double meaning (wanted as in sought by police vs. wanted as in desired by parents), delivered by Gob with complete tactlessness
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'You weren't even a wanted child' works as: (1) a logical argument against Buster being a wanted criminal, (2) a devastating childhood indictment, (3) a callback to the Bluth parents' general neglect. Gob delivers it as pure logical argument, not cruelty — which makes it darker. The economy is near-perfect (six words that do enormous work). The double meaning is immediate but layered. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Buster asks Gob to keep his secret ('please don't tell him') about still pretending to be a fugitive — unaware Gob himself is hiding a dead body
jokes.setup: Buster asks Gob to keep his secret that he knows he's not really on the lam
jokes.punchline: Gob agrees — 'Your secret is safe with me. No strings attached. If... you help me get rid of this big, dead-body-shaped thing.'
jokes.explanation: The 'No strings attached. If...' structure is classically Gob — the fake-out of charitable promise followed immediately by transactional demand. 'Dead-body-shaped thing' is a great euphemism that is both suspicious and deniable. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Your secret is safe with me, Buster. No strings attached. If... you help me get rid of this big, dead-body-shaped thing.'
jokes.setup: Gob promises to keep Buster's secret unconditionally, then immediately attaches a condition
jokes.punchline: 'No strings attached. If...' — the immediate withdrawal of 'no strings' followed by a request involving a 'dead-body-shaped thing'
jokes.explanation: The 'no strings attached... if' structure is a classic misdirect, but 'dead-body-shaped thing' is the unexpected escalation that makes it exceptional. Gob's attempt to maintain plausible deniability while clearly describing a body ('I just told you, it's... Mm. It's from a magician friend') is deeply character-consistent. The magician's final disappearing act callback sets up the 'On the Next' segment. Strong structure, dark escalation, very Gob.

jokes.text: Gob explains the dead-body-shaped thing is 'from a magician friend. It was part of his big... his big final farewell disappearing act.'
jokes.setup: Buster asks what the dead-body-shaped thing is
jokes.punchline: 'His big final farewell disappearing act' — the euphemism implies the magician is dead inside the prop, with 'final farewell' doing the work of 'he died,' delivered with Gob's characteristic evasiveness
jokes.explanation: 'Final farewell disappearing act' as a euphemism for death-inside-a-magic-prop is elegantly structured. The pauses ('his big... his big final farewell') suggest Gob searching for the most charitable description. Dark, character-specific, and sets up the season arc.

jokes.text: 'Need I remind you, Buster, that as my one-time assistant, you are still bound by the magician's assistant oath.'
jokes.setup: Gob needs Buster's help disposing of something suspicious
jokes.punchline: He invokes 'the magician's assistant oath' as a binding legal/ethical obligation — implying this is a formal institution with enforceable oaths
jokes.explanation: The 'magician's assistant oath' is a perfect Gob invention — he takes his profession with profound seriousness while everyone around him treats it as a joke. The formality of invoking it ('Need I remind you...') implies a whole institutional framework Gob has constructed around magic. 'Say no more' as Buster's response — immediately accepting the oath's validity — is a great button.

jokes.text: 'On the Next': Gob arrives for 'heterosexual conversion therapy' — 'My name is Gob Bluth. I'm here for a 12:30 un-gaying.'
jokes.setup: The 'On the Next' segment previews Gob's Gay Mafia obligation: conversion therapy
jokes.punchline: 'A 12:30 un-gaying' — Gob's appointment language treats sexual orientation like a spa service
jokes.explanation: 'Un-gaying' as a service-desk appointment is extraordinary economy — one invented word that encapsulates the absurdity of conversion therapy, Gob's businesslike approach to his sexuality crisis, and the show's satirical stance. The '12:30' specificity makes it feel like a calendar invite. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Hey, Gob. It's me, Ann's mother.' / 'Who?' / 'That's the one.'
jokes.setup: Ann's mother introduces herself at the conversion therapy center
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'Who?' — a callback to the running 'Her?' / dismissal of Ann gag spanning multiple seasons — and Ann's mother's flat 'That's the one' confirms he doesn't remember Ann despite having been engaged to her
jokes.explanation: The 'Ann? Her?' running gag is one of AD's most beloved, and this deployment is exceptional: Ann's own mother gets the 'Who?' and responds with 'That's the one' — confirming that Gob's forgetting of Ann is so complete that even her mother has accepted it as his identifying characteristic. The economy is extraordinary (six words across three lines). One of the highest-craft moments in the episode. Justifies 8.5+.

jokes.text: Conversion therapy waiver: 'Just some legalese stating that if you kill yourself as a result of the therapy, it's your fault, not ours.'
jokes.setup: Gob is signing in for conversion therapy
jokes.punchline: The intake paperwork includes explicit suicide liability waiver, delivered in cheerful 'legalese' framing as though this is standard business practice
jokes.explanation: Dark satire of conversion therapy presented with bureaucratic casualness — 'just some legalese' normalizing a suicide waiver. The deadpan delivery of something genuinely sinister is sharp political comedy. The 'not ours' as a legal finality is the button.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Boy, wish I could pray that sweater away.'
jokes.setup: Gob is at conversion therapy, supposedly there to become straight; Ann's mother is present
jokes.punchline: Gob's comment about praying away Ann's mother's sweater — riffing on 'pray the gay away' — is both a commentary on conversion therapy and a demonstration that he's already objectifying a woman (a sign of his 'success' in the therapy's terms, or a sign of his hopelessness)
jokes.explanation: 'Wish I could pray that sweater away' is a beautifully constructed line: it riffs on 'pray the gay away,' reframes Gob's attraction as fashion criticism (or genuine attraction?), and the target being Ann's mother adds an extra layer of awkwardness. The economy is exceptional — one sentence that works on four levels simultaneously. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Unfortunately for Gob... it works a little too well.'
jokes.setup: Gob has arrived determined to prove conversion therapy doesn't work
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals it 'works a little too well' — setting up a future plotline where Gob is accidentally straight-converted
jokes.explanation: A strong 'On the Next' button. The 'too well' qualifier is the joke — conversion therapy being portrayed as accidentally effective (and therefore terrible in the other direction) is absurdist and pointed satire. The 'unfortunately' framing is good irony.

jokes.text: Buster uses the 'dead body in the truck' as a step stool to drop something off for Gob — and the truck has 'sticky stairs' with a 'blood-like substance'
jokes.setup: Buster drops off something for Gob using the getaway truck
jokes.punchline: The stairs on the truck are 'pretty sticky' and there's 'a blood-like substance back there' — Buster observes this with the curiosity of a person who has absolutely no idea he's just distributed evidence of a crime, treating it as a 'tomorrow job'
jokes.explanation: 'That's a tomorrow job. That's for the daytime.' — Buster's cheerful deferral of blood evidence cleanup is a perfect dark-comedy button to the episode. His obliviousness about what 'the blood-like substance' actually is, combined with treating crime-scene cleanup as a household chore, is deeply in character and darkly funny. The specificity of 'tomorrow job' as a category is highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'I wonder if there's, like, a really tall car wash around here.'
jokes.setup: The getaway truck has sticky, blood-covered stairs
jokes.punchline: Oscar's practical solution is a 'really tall car wash' — applying mundane domestic logic to a crime scene cleanup problem, unaware of what they're actually cleaning
jokes.explanation: 'A really tall car wash' is a wonderfully mundane solution to what is presumably evidence disposal. The specificity ('really tall') implies Oscar's mind went to the practical engineering challenge rather than the moral one. Economical and funny.

tmdb_overview: As George Sr. attempts to win back Lucille, Michael assembles a mold of their scheme. Meanwhile, Buster gets caught up in Tobias and Gob's business.
insight: Gob's immigration scheme generates 57 jokes in 57 minutes—character comedy sustains what plot alone couldn't.
